explanation.summary: Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line treatment for OSA in children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. It improves daytime behavior, decreases sleepiness, and increases quality of life.
explanation.whyCorrect: In children, the most common cause of OSA is adenotonsillar hypertrophy. Adenotonsillectomy is the recommended first-line therapy because it directly addresses the anatomical cause of obstruction and has been shown to improve symptoms, behavior, and quality of life even when hypertrophy is not the sole contributing factor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CPAP is the first-line therapy for adults with OSA, not children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. In pediatric patients, surgery is preferred when a surgically correctable cause is identified.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intranasal corticosteroids may have a role as adjunctive therapy in mild pediatric OSA, but they are not first-line treatment when significant adenotonsillar hypertrophy is present.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mandibular advancement devices are used in adults with mild to moderate OSA. They are not appropriate for a 4-year-old child with adenotonsillar hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Weight loss counseling is relevant for obese patients. This child's OSA is caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy, which requires surgical correction.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with newly diagnosed obstructive sleep apnea asks why his physician is concerned about his blood pressure. His polysomnography showed an AHI of 25 with oxygen desaturation to 78% during events. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which OSA contributes to systemic hypertension?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased cardiac output during apneic episodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Persistent hypercapnia suppressing the renin-angiotensin system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Upper airway inflammation extending to the vasculature

explanation.summary: Recurrent episodes of intermittent hypoxia during obstructive events trigger sympathetic nervous system activation, which leads to systemic vasoconstriction and contributes to the development of sustained hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Repetitive cycles of hypoxia and reoxygenation during obstructive apneas activate the sympathetic nervous system through peripheral chemoreceptor stimulation. This results in elevated catecholamine levels, vasoconstriction, and an inflammatory response that collectively contribute to the development of sustained systemic hypertension, even during waking hours.

whyWrongByChoice.A: OSA causes intermittent hypoxia and hypercapnia, not chronic respiratory alkalosis. Hyperventilation is not a feature of obstructive sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While cardiac output may transiently decrease during severe apneic episodes, the primary mechanism of hypertension in OSA is sympathetically mediated vasoconstriction from intermittent hypoxia, not reduced cardiac output.
whyWrongByChoice.D: OSA is associated with intermittent hypercapnia during apneas, but this does not suppress the renin-angiotensin system. In fact, sympathetic activation tends to stimulate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While OSA is associated with systemic inflammation, the primary mechanism of hypertension is sympathetic nervous system activation from intermittent hypoxia, not direct extension of upper airway inflammation to blood vessels.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and morning headaches. She denies snoring but her husband reports frequent pauses in breathing during sleep. She has a BMI of 23 kg/m² and a normal oropharyngeal examination. Her medical history is significant for a brainstem stroke 6 months ago. A sleep study shows frequent apneas without respiratory effort. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Apneas without respiratory effort on polysomnography indicate central sleep apnea. Her history of brainstem stroke is a recognized cause of CSA due to impaired central respiratory drive.
explanation.whyCorrect: Central sleep apnea is characterized by cessation of airflow without respiratory effort, distinguishing it from OSA where effort against a closed airway is present. Brainstem strokes can damage the respiratory centers, impairing the CNS drive to breathe and causing CSA. Her normal BMI and oropharynx further argue against an obstructive etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Narcolepsy causes excessive daytime sleepiness, but it is characterized by cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, and sleep paralysis, not apneic episodes without respiratory effort on polysomnography.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA is characterized by apneas with continued respiratory effort against a collapsed upper airway. This patient has apneas without respiratory effort, which defines central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome requires obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) with chronic daytime hypercapnia. This patient has a normal BMI of 23 kg/m².
whyWrongByChoice.E: Upper airway resistance syndrome involves increased respiratory effort against a partially obstructed airway causing arousals, not apneas without respiratory effort.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a BMI of 42 kg/m² presents with worsening dyspnea, daytime somnolence, and lower extremity edema. Arterial blood gas on room air shows pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 52 mm Hg, PaO₂ 58 mm Hg, and HCO₃⁻ 30 mEq/L. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (Pickwickian syndrome) is defined by obesity (BMI ≥30), chronic daytime hypoventilation (PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg), and hypoxemia that cannot be attributed to another condition. The metabolic compensation (elevated HCO₃⁻) confirms chronicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient meets all criteria for obesity hypoventilation syndrome: morbid obesity (BMI 42), chronic daytime hypercapnia (PaCO₂ 52), and hypoxemia (PaO₂ 58). The elevated bicarbonate indicates metabolic compensation for chronic respiratory acidosis. The restrictive pattern on PFTs is consistent with decreased chest wall compliance from obesity. Lower extremity edema suggests developing cor pulmonale.

whyWrongByChoice.A: COPD causes an obstructive pattern on PFTs (decreased FEV1/FVC ratio), not the restrictive pattern seen in this patient. COPD is typically associated with smoking history, not morbid obesity as the primary finding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While heart failure can cause dyspnea and edema, it does not typically present with chronic hypercapnia and a restrictive pattern on PFTs. The constellation of obesity, hypercapnia, and hypoxemia points to OHS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis causes a restrictive pattern but typically presents with fine bibasilar crackles and does not cause chronic hypercapnia in early or moderate stages. It is not associated with morbid obesity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary embolism causes acute hypoxemia and hypocapnia (from hyperventilation), not chronic hypercapnia with metabolic compensation.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is being evaluated for resistant hypertension despite adherence to three antihypertensive medications. His wife reports that he snores loudly and sometimes appears to stop breathing during sleep. He is obese with a BMI of 33 kg/m². Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of his resistant hypertension?

explanation.summary: Obstructive sleep apnea is a common and often underrecognized cause of secondary (resistant) hypertension. The combination of snoring, witnessed apneas, and obesity strongly suggests OSA as the etiology.
explanation.whyCorrect: OSA is one of the most common causes of resistant hypertension. Intermittent hypoxia during obstructive events triggers sustained sympathetic nervous system activation and inflammatory responses that contribute to hypertension refractory to standard pharmacotherapy. This patient has classic OSA risk factors and symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cushing syndrome can cause hypertension but typically presents with additional features such as central obesity, moon facies, striae, muscle weakness, and glucose intolerance, none of which are described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pheochromocytoma causes episodic or sustained hypertension with paroxysmal headaches, diaphoresis, and palpitations. The clinical presentation here is more consistent with OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary hyperaldosteronism causes resistant hypertension and should be considered, but the presence of loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and obesity makes OSA the most likely diagnosis in this clinical context.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal artery stenosis can cause resistant hypertension, especially in elderly patients with atherosclerosis, but there is no mention of an abdominal bruit or renal insufficiency. The sleep-related symptoms point to OSA.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man undergoes polysomnography for suspected sleep apnea. The study reveals 22 episodes of complete cessation of airflow lasting at least 10 seconds each per hour of sleep, all associated with continued chest and abdominal wall movement. Which of the following best characterizes these findings?

explanation.summary: Complete cessation of airflow with continued respiratory effort (chest and abdominal movement) defines obstructive apneas. An AHI of 22 falls in the moderate range (15-30 events/hour).
explanation.whyCorrect: The presence of continued respiratory effort (chest and abdominal wall movement) despite absent airflow is the hallmark of obstructive apneas, distinguishing them from central apneas where effort is absent. An AHI of 22 events per hour classifies as moderate severity (mild: 5-15, moderate: 15-30, severe: >30).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Central apneas are characterized by absence of both airflow and respiratory effort. This patient has continued chest and abdominal movement, indicating the obstruction is in the upper airway, not a failure of central drive.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mixed apneas have an initial central component (no effort) followed by an obstructive component (effort against obstruction). All events in this study show continuous respiratory effort, consistent with purely obstructive apneas. Additionally, 22/hour is moderate, not mild.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Severe OSA is defined as an AHI greater than 30 events per hour. This patient has an AHI of 22, which falls in the moderate range (15-30).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypopneas are partial reductions in airflow, not complete cessation. These events involve complete cessation of airflow, which defines apneas.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with heart failure (NYHA class III) and central sleep apnea has persistent symptoms despite optimization of heart failure therapy. His sleep study shows an AHI of 35 with predominantly central apneas. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for his sleep-disordered breathing?

explanation.summary: CPAP is the initial positive airway pressure therapy recommended for central sleep apnea associated with heart failure, as it can reduce central apneas and improve cardiac function.
explanation.whyCorrect: For patients with CSA due to heart failure, CPAP is recommended as the initial positive pressure therapy. CPAP reduces preload and afterload, which can improve cardiac function while also stabilizing ventilation. Optimization of heart failure management is the first priority, but when CSA persists, CPAP is the next step.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acetazolamide has been studied for central sleep apnea at altitude but is not a standard first-line therapy for CSA in heart failure patients. It can cause metabolic acidosis and has limited evidence in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.B: BiPAP may be considered for CSA patients who fail CPAP or who have persistent nocturnal hypoventilation (eg, after stroke), but CPAP is the initial therapy to try in heart failure-related CSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mandibular advancement devices are designed for obstructive sleep apnea, where they prevent pharyngeal collapse by advancing the mandible. They have no role in central sleep apnea where the problem is central respiratory drive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Supplemental oxygen alone may partially reduce central apneas, but it does not address the underlying ventilatory instability. Positive airway pressure therapy is more effective for CSA in heart failure.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a BMI of 24 kg/m² presents with daytime fatigue and unrefreshing sleep. She denies snoring. Her husband confirms she does not snore. She takes oxycodone daily for chronic back pain. A sleep study reveals an AHI of 18, with apneas occurring without respiratory effort. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of her sleep apnea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased pharyngeal muscle tone from opioids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fat deposition narrowing the upper airway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opioid-induced suppression of central respiratory drive

explanation.summary: This patient has central sleep apnea caused by chronic opioid use. Opioids suppress brainstem respiratory centers, leading to apneas without respiratory effort, which is the hallmark finding on polysomnography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Opioids are a well-established cause of central sleep apnea. They suppress the brainstem respiratory centers, reducing the central drive to breathe, particularly during sleep. The polysomnography finding of apneas without respiratory effort confirms the central nature. Her normal BMI, absent snoring, and lack of anatomical abnormalities argue against obstructive causes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While opioids can affect muscle tone, decreased pharyngeal muscle tone would produce obstructive apneas with continued respiratory effort. This patient has apneas without effort, indicating a central mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fat deposition narrowing the upper airway causes obstructive sleep apnea. This patient has a normal BMI of 24, and her apneas are without respiratory effort, indicating a central rather than obstructive etiology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Retrognathia causes a posteriorly displaced mandible that narrows the pharyngeal airway, leading to obstructive sleep apnea with respiratory effort. This patient's apneas are central in nature.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tonsillar hypertrophy causes obstructive sleep apnea by physically narrowing the oropharynx. It produces apneas with respiratory effort and is associated with snoring, neither of which is present here.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with moderate obstructive sleep apnea (AHI 20) cannot tolerate CPAP despite multiple mask adjustments. He has a BMI of 28 kg/m² and normal tonsils. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative therapy?

explanation.summary: Mandibular advancement devices (oral appliances) are the recommended alternative for patients with mild to moderate OSA who cannot tolerate CPAP. They advance the mandible forward, enlarging the pharyngeal airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral appliances such as mandibular advancement devices are recommended for patients with mild to moderate OSA who fail or cannot tolerate CPAP. They work by advancing the mandible and tongue forward, increasing the space behind the tongue and soft palate to reduce airway collapse during sleep.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line treatment for children with OSA due to adenotonsillar hypertrophy. This adult patient has normal tonsils, so this surgery would not be beneficial.
whyWrongByChoice.B: BiPAP provides bilevel pressures and is used for central sleep apnea or patients requiring higher inspiratory pressures. It is not the typical next step for CPAP intolerance in OSA; the issue is mask tolerance, not pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypoglossal nerve stimulation is an emerging therapy for selected patients with moderate to severe OSA who fail CPAP and meet specific criteria. An oral appliance trial is typically attempted first for moderate OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tracheostomy is a last-resort treatment for life-threatening OSA refractory to all other therapies. It is not appropriate for moderate OSA with CPAP intolerance when less invasive options remain.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with untreated obstructive sleep apnea presents to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident. He reports falling asleep at the wheel. His wife has been urging him to seek treatment for his loud snoring. Which of the following complications of untreated OSA is this event an example of?

explanation.summary: Untreated OSA causes fragmented sleep due to recurrent arousals from apneic events, leading to excessive daytime sleepiness that significantly increases the risk of motor vehicle accidents.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excessive daytime sleepiness is a major consequence of the fragmented sleep caused by recurrent apneas and arousals in untreated OSA. Patients with untreated OSA have a significantly increased risk of drowsy driving and motor vehicle accidents, which is one of the most dangerous complications of the disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While OSA is associated with cardiac arrhythmias, the patient specifically reports falling asleep at the wheel, which is consistent with excessive daytime sleepiness rather than arrhythmia-induced syncope.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypoxia-induced seizures would present with convulsive activity, not falling asleep. The intermittent nocturnal hypoxia of OSA does not typically cause daytime seizures.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Narcolepsy is a separate sleep disorder characterized by cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations. This patient's sleepiness is attributed to untreated OSA with typical symptoms of loud snoring.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transient ischemic attacks cause focal neurological deficits, not gradual sleep onset while driving. While OSA increases stroke risk, this event is due to sleepiness from fragmented sleep.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with obstructive sleep apnea treated with CPAP for 3 years presents for routine follow-up. His wife reports that his snoring has resolved and he no longer has apneic episodes. However, he continues to experience morning headaches. An arterial blood gas shows PaCO₂ of 50 mm Hg while awake. His BMI is 38 kg/m². Which of the following is the most likely explanation for his persistent symptoms?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tension-type headache unrelated to sleep apnea

explanation.summary: This patient's morning headaches and daytime hypercapnia despite well-controlled OSA suggest coexisting obesity hypoventilation syndrome, which is characterized by obesity with chronic daytime hypercapnia (PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg) not explained by another condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) is defined by obesity (BMI ≥30), chronic awake alveolar hypoventilation (PaCO₂ >45 mm Hg), and sleep-disordered breathing not attributable to another cause. OHS frequently coexists with OSA. Despite adequate CPAP controlling his obstructive events, the chronic hypercapnia and morning headaches (from CO₂ retention) indicate OHS requiring additional management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP machine malfunction would result in recurrent obstructive events, snoring, and witnessed apneas. The wife confirms these symptoms have resolved, suggesting the CPAP is functioning properly.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While central sleep apnea can coexist with OSA, the key finding here is awake hypercapnia (PaCO₂ 50 mm Hg), which is more consistent with obesity hypoventilation syndrome than isolated CSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inadequate CPAP pressure would fail to resolve the snoring and apneic episodes, which are reported as resolved by the wife. The persistent symptoms are due to a separate coexisting condition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Morning headaches in the context of awake hypercapnia are most likely due to CO₂ retention causing cerebral vasodilation, not a coincidental tension headache. The finding should not be dismissed.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is referred for a sleep study. He has a BMI of 32 kg/m² and reports loud snoring. The sleep study can be performed either in a sleep laboratory or at home. Which of the following measurements is available with in-laboratory polysomnography but typically NOT with home sleep apnea testing?

explanation.summary: Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) devices typically do not include electroencephalography (EEG), which means they cannot reliably detect arousals from sleep or determine sleep stages, unlike in-laboratory polysomnography.
explanation.whyCorrect: In-laboratory polysomnography includes EEG monitoring, which allows detection of sleep stages, arousals, and respiratory effort-related arousals (RERAs). Most home sleep apnea testing devices do not include EEG, so they cannot reliably identify sleep stages or arousals, which may result in underestimation of the AHI since total sleep time cannot be accurately determined.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Airflow measurement is a core component of both in-laboratory PSG and HSAT devices, as it is essential for detecting apneas and hypopneas in any sleep study.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heart rate monitoring is typically included in both in-laboratory and home sleep testing through pulse oximetry or ECG leads.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxygen saturation measurement via pulse oximetry is a standard component of both in-laboratory PSG and HSAT devices.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Many HSAT devices include respiratory effort bands, although the quality may vary. EEG is the major monitoring modality absent from most home devices.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation and congestive heart failure presents with worsening daytime sleepiness. His wife describes a pattern where his breathing becomes progressively deeper, then shallower, followed by a pause, in a repeating cycle throughout the night. Which of the following pathophysiological mechanisms best explains this breathing pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collapse of pharyngeal muscles during REM sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed circulatory time affecting chemoreceptor feedback

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diaphragmatic fatigue from chronic heart failure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased sensitivity to oxygen at peripheral chemoreceptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vagal nerve stimulation from atrial distension

explanation.summary: In heart failure, prolonged circulatory time delays the transmission of blood gas information from the lungs to central chemoreceptors, creating an overshoot-undershoot pattern of ventilation that manifests as Cheyne-Stokes respiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heart failure causes prolonged circulation time, meaning changes in blood gas tensions at the pulmonary capillaries take longer to reach the brainstem chemoreceptors. This delay creates a feedback loop instability: by the time the brain detects rising PaCO₂, the resulting hyperventilation overshoots, dropping PaCO₂ below the apneic threshold and causing apnea. The cycle then repeats, producing Cheyne-Stokes breathing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pharyngeal muscle collapse during REM sleep is the mechanism of obstructive sleep apnea. This patient has a crescendo-decrescendo breathing pattern consistent with central sleep apnea, not airway obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diaphragmatic fatigue does not produce a cyclical crescendo-decrescendo breathing pattern. Heart failure-associated CSA is due to ventilatory control instability from delayed chemoreceptor feedback.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The key chemoreceptor involved in CSA with heart failure is the central (medullary) CO₂ chemoreceptor, not the peripheral oxygen chemoreceptors. The mechanism involves sensitivity to PaCO₂ fluctuations with delayed feedback.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While atrial distension occurs in heart failure, vagal stimulation from atrial stretch does not cause the periodic breathing pattern described. The mechanism is delayed circulatory feedback to chemoreceptors.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with severe obstructive sleep apnea (AHI 45) has been on CPAP for 6 months but reports the mask is uncomfortable and he removes it after 2 hours nightly. He drinks 3 beers every evening before bed and takes lorazepam for anxiety. Which of the following recommendations is most likely to improve his sleep apnea symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add supplemental oxygen to the CPAP circuit

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discontinue alcohol and sedative use before sleep

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase CPAP pressure to compensate for short use

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replace CPAP with a mandibular advancement device

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch to an auto-titrating CPAP device

explanation.summary: Alcohol and benzodiazepines (lorazepam) decrease pharyngeal muscle tone and suppress arousal responses, worsening OSA. Discontinuing these substances before sleep is a critical lifestyle modification that can significantly improve symptoms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both alcohol and benzodiazepines worsen OSA by decreasing upper airway muscle tone, promoting pharyngeal collapse, and increasing the arousal threshold. Discontinuing these substances is a key lifestyle intervention that patients with OSA should be counseled about, as it can reduce the severity and frequency of obstructive events.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen does not address the upper airway obstruction in OSA. While it may improve oxygen saturation, it does not prevent apneas. Addressing modifiable risk factors like alcohol and sedatives is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increasing CPAP pressure does not compensate for limited use duration. The focus should be on improving CPAP adherence and addressing modifiable factors like alcohol and sedative use that worsen his OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mandibular advancement devices are alternatives for mild to moderate OSA. With severe OSA (AHI 45), CPAP remains the preferred therapy. The priority is addressing modifiable worsening factors before changing the treatment modality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While auto-titrating CPAP may improve comfort for some patients, addressing the use of alcohol and benzodiazepines, which are actively worsening his OSA and potentially contributing to CPAP intolerance, should be the first step.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with obstructive sleep apnea undergoes an echocardiogram that reveals right ventricular dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, and an estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 55 mm Hg. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiological link between his sleep apnea and these cardiac findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic intermittent hypoxia causing pulmonary vasoconstriction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left ventricular diastolic dysfunction from obesity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nocturnal hypertension causing aortic root dilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent Valsalva maneuvers increasing right atrial pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systemic inflammatory response damaging the tricuspid valve

explanation.summary: Chronic intermittent hypoxia from recurrent obstructive apneas causes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, which over time leads to pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular dilation, and cor pulmonale.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent episodes of hypoxia during obstructive apneas trigger pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction via hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV). Chronic exposure leads to pulmonary vascular remodeling and sustained pulmonary hypertension. The increased right ventricular afterload results in RV dilation and tricuspid regurgitation, a condition known as cor pulmonale.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While obesity commonly coexists with OSA and can contribute to cardiac dysfunction, the direct pathophysiological link between OSA and right heart findings is through pulmonary vasoconstriction from intermittent hypoxia, not left ventricular diastolic dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA does cause nocturnal blood pressure surges, but these primarily affect the systemic circulation. The right heart findings are due to pulmonary hypertension from hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, not aortic root pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While negative intrathoracic pressure swings occur during obstructive events, these are not Valsalva maneuvers. The primary mechanism for right heart dysfunction is pulmonary hypertension from chronic hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: OSA is associated with systemic inflammation, but this does not cause direct structural damage to the tricuspid valve. The tricuspid regurgitation is functional, resulting from right ventricular dilation due to pulmonary hypertension.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with a BMI of 26 kg/m² presents with excessive daytime sleepiness. She has a small, posteriorly displaced mandible noted on physical examination. Her husband reports loud snoring. Polysomnography reveals an AHI of 12. Which of the following anatomical findings is most directly contributing to her condition?

explanation.summary: Retrognathia (posteriorly displaced mandible) narrows the retroglossal airway space, predisposing to pharyngeal collapse during sleep. This is a recognized structural risk factor for OSA, especially in non-obese patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Retrognathia causes posterior displacement of the mandible and tongue base, reducing the retroglossal airway space and predisposing to upper airway collapse during sleep. In this non-obese patient, the small, posteriorly displaced mandible identified on examination is the most likely anatomical contributor to her OSA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A deviated nasal septum can contribute to nasal obstruction and may worsen snoring, but it is not the primary mechanism causing pharyngeal collapse and obstructive apneas in a patient with documented retrognathia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An enlarged thyroid gland (goiter) can cause external airway compression but is not described in this patient. The posteriorly displaced mandible is the identified anatomical abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Macroglossia (enlarged tongue) can contribute to airway obstruction, but the examination specifically identifies retrognathia as the structural abnormality, making it the most direct contributor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tonsillar hypertrophy is the most common cause of OSA in children, but this patient has retrognathia identified on exam, and no mention is made of enlarged tonsils.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with hypothyroidism and a BMI of 36 kg/m² is diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea. He asks his physician why sleeping on his back makes his symptoms worse. Which of the following best explains the positional worsening of his OSA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased venous return in the supine position

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gravity causes posterior tongue displacement in the supine position

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased chest wall compliance when supine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced diaphragmatic excursion from abdominal contents

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stimulation of the vagus nerve compressing the airway

explanation.summary: In the supine position, gravity pulls the tongue and soft palate posteriorly toward the pharyngeal wall, narrowing the upper airway and increasing the likelihood of obstruction during sleep.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a patient sleeps supine, gravity causes the tongue, soft palate, and other pharyngeal soft tissues to fall posteriorly toward the posterior pharyngeal wall. This narrows the already compromised upper airway in patients with OSA, increasing the frequency and severity of obstructive events. This is why positional therapy (avoiding supine sleep) can be beneficial.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Venous return actually increases in the supine position due to elimination of gravitational effects on blood pooling. This is not the mechanism for worsening of OSA in the supine position.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chest wall compliance is not significantly altered by position. The worsening of OSA in the supine position is due to gravitational effects on upper airway soft tissues, not changes in chest wall mechanics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While abdominal contents can reduce diaphragmatic excursion in obese patients, this affects ventilation generally. The specific positional worsening of OSA is primarily due to gravitational posterior displacement of the tongue and palate obstructing the pharynx.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The vagus nerve does not compress the airway. The positional worsening is mechanical, caused by gravity pulling pharyngeal soft tissues posteriorly in the supine position.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with acromegaly presents for evaluation of excessive daytime sleepiness and witnessed apneas. Her BMI is 27 kg/m². Polysomnography confirms obstructive sleep apnea with an AHI of 28. Which of the following features of acromegaly most directly contributes to her obstructive sleep apnea?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Growth hormone excess causing insulin resistance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Macroglossia and soft tissue hypertrophy of the pharynx

explanation.summary: Acromegaly causes excessive growth hormone, leading to soft tissue hypertrophy throughout the body, including macroglossia and pharyngeal tissue enlargement, which narrow the upper airway and predispose to obstructive sleep apnea.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acromegaly is associated with OSA due to excess growth hormone causing macroglossia (enlarged tongue) and hypertrophy of pharyngeal soft tissues. These anatomical changes narrow the upper airway, making it more susceptible to collapse during sleep. This explains OSA in this patient with a relatively normal BMI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While acromegaly can cause cardiomyopathy, fluid redistribution is not the primary mechanism of OSA in acromegaly. The direct cause is soft tissue hypertrophy of the upper airway.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin resistance in acromegaly contributes to metabolic complications but does not directly cause upper airway obstruction during sleep.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Peripheral neuropathy affecting respiratory muscles would more likely contribute to central hypoventilation, not obstructive sleep apnea. The obstruction in this case is due to anatomical changes in the pharynx.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A pituitary adenoma causing acromegaly does not compress the brainstem, which is located in the posterior fossa. The pituitary gland is in the sella turcica, far from the brainstem respiratory centers.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe congestive heart failure and central sleep apnea is being treated with CPAP but continues to have an elevated AHI with predominantly central events. His cardiologist optimizes his heart failure medications, but central apneas persist. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing his sleep-disordered breathing?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for phrenic nerve stimulator placement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Switch to bilevel positive airway pressure

explanation.summary: For central sleep apnea patients with heart failure who fail CPAP despite optimized cardiac therapy, bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) is an appropriate next step, as it provides higher inspiratory pressures to support ventilation during central apneas.
explanation.whyCorrect: When CSA persists despite optimized heart failure therapy and adequate CPAP trial, bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) is an appropriate escalation. BiPAP delivers higher pressure during inspiration and lower pressure during expiration, which can more effectively support ventilation during central apneic events by augmenting tidal volume when central respiratory drive is insufficient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen alone does not adequately address central apneas. While it may improve oxygenation, it does not provide ventilatory support during periods of absent respiratory drive.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Discontinuing positive airway pressure therapy would remove the ventilatory support this patient needs during central apneas, likely worsening hypoxemia and sleep quality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phrenic nerve stimulation is an emerging therapy for CSA but is not yet the standard of care. BiPAP should be trialed before considering more invasive interventions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty is a surgical treatment for obstructive sleep apnea that removes pharyngeal soft tissue. It has no role in central sleep apnea, which is caused by impaired central respiratory drive.
